subtopic: Infant with non-bilious projectile vomiting and electrolyte disturbance
stem: A 5-week-old first-born male is brought to the emergency department with a 10-day history of vomiting after every feed, now forceful and arching over the shoulder. The vomit is milky, never green or blood-stained. He is hungry immediately after vomiting and feeds vigorously. Birth was at 39+4 by NVD, weight 3.6 kg, uneventful pregnancy. Today his weight is 3.5 kg (50th to 3rd centile cross), capillary refill 3 seconds, anterior fontanelle slightly sunken, and a 1 cm mobile firm mass is palpable in the right epigastrium after a test feed. Visible peristalsis crosses the upper abdomen from left to right. Venous blood gas shows pH 7.51, pCO2 50 mmHg, HCO3 38 mmol/L, Na 134 mmol/L, K 3.1 mmol/L, Cl 89 mmol/L, glucose 4.2 mmol/L.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 168 bpm, RR 44, T 36.8 degC, SpO2 98%, weight 3.5 kg.
data_table.Examination: Sunken fontanelle, CRT 3 s, mucous membranes dry. Soft abdomen, palpable olive RUQ, visible peristalsis.
data_table.Investigations: VBG: pH 7.51, pCO2 50, HCO3 38, Na 134, K 3.1, Cl 89, glucose 4.2 mmol/L.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Proceed immediately to theatre for Ramstedt pyloromyotomy after a single 20 mL/kg saline bolus
options.rationale: Pyloric stenosis is never a surgical emergency. Operating on an alkalotic, hypokalaemic, hypochloraemic infant risks post-anaesthetic apnoea from compensatory CSF alkalosis. The deficit must be corrected first (RCH CPG Pyloric Stenosis).

options.text: Resuscitate with 0.9% sodium chloride 20 mL/kg, then commence 0.45% saline with 5% dextrose and added potassium at 1.5 times maintenance until chloride is greater than 100 mmol/L and bicarbonate less than 30 mmol/L, then arrange pyloromyotomy
options.rationale: Hypochloraemic hypokalaemic metabolic alkalosis must be corrected before pyloromyotomy. Targets: Cl greater than 100 mmol/L, HCO3 less than 30 mmol/L, K greater than 3.5 mmol/L. Ultrasound is the confirmatory investigation (pyloric muscle thickness greater than 3 mm, channel length greater than 14 mm) (RCH CPG; Therapeutic Guidelines: Paediatric Fluids).
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines: Paediatric Fluid and Electrolyte

options.text: Commence omeprazole 1 mg/kg orally and review in clinic in one week
options.rationale: Projectile non-bilious vomiting with a palpable olive and hypochloraemic alkalosis is pyloric stenosis, not GORD. PPIs do not treat mechanical outflow obstruction (RCH CPG GORD).

options.text: Request an urgent upper GI contrast study to exclude malrotation before any fluid resuscitation
options.rationale: Vomiting is non-bilious and clinical features are diagnostic of pyloric stenosis. Upper GI contrast is the investigation for suspected malrotation (bilious vomiting). Ultrasound is the investigation here, and only after fluid resuscitation is started (RCH CPG).

options.text: Trial a hydrolysed formula for two weeks as cow's milk protein allergy is the most likely diagnosis
options.rationale: CMPA in this age usually presents with mucousy or bloody stools, eczema, or non-projectile vomiting, and does not produce a hypochloraemic alkalosis or a palpable pyloric mass (ASCIA CMPA Guidelines).

explanation.summary: Pyloric stenosis (3-6 weeks, male predominant 4:1, first-born) produces hypertrophy of the pyloric muscle, gastric outlet obstruction, and loss of HCl in vomit. The resulting hypochloraemic hypokalaemic metabolic alkalosis must be corrected before pyloromyotomy.
key_points: Classic age 3-6 weeks, M:F 4:1, first-born predominance
Non-bilious projectile vomiting, hungry vomiter, weight loss
VBG: low Cl, low K, high HCO3, alkalotic pH
USS criteria: muscle thickness greater than 3 mm AND channel length greater than 14 mm
Correct fluids and electrolytes BEFORE theatre to avoid post-op apnoea
explanation.pearls: If the infant is alkalotic, do not let them go to theatre. The anaesthetist will hand them back.

sources.label: RCH Clinical Practice Guideline: Pyloric Stenosis
Therapeutic Guidelines: Paediatric Fluid and Electrolyte

subtopic: Toddler with intermittent colicky pain and bloody stool
stem: A 14-month-old previously well boy presents with an 8-hour history of episodic screaming in 15-20 minute cycles, drawing his knees up to his chest, with pallor and limpness between episodes. His mother describes one stool 90 minutes ago that was dark red and mucousy, like jam. There has been one episode of non-bilious vomiting. He had a viral URTI 5 days ago. There has been no foreign travel and no unwell contacts. On examination he is now quiet and pale, HR 152, CRT 3 seconds, T 37.1 degC. The abdomen is soft with a vague sausage-shaped mass palpable in the right upper quadrant and an empty right iliac fossa. There is no peritonism, no guarding, no rebound. Bedside ultrasound performed by the paediatric registrar shows a target sign in the right upper quadrant with concentric rings of bowel wall. A surgical registrar and an interventional radiologist are both immediately available.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 152 bpm, RR 32, BP 92/58, T 37.1 degC, SpO2 99%, CRT 3 s.
data_table.Examination: Pale, lethargic between episodes. Sausage mass RUQ, empty RIF (Dance sign). No peritonism, no guarding.
data_table.Investigations: POCUS: target sign RUQ, concentric rings of bowel-within-bowel.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Reassure and arrange outpatient paediatric review tomorrow given the absence of peritonism
options.rationale: Confirmed intussusception with red-currant stool requires immediate reduction. Delay risks bowel ischaemia and perforation (RCH CPG Intussusception).

options.text: Establish IV access with two large-bore cannulae, send group and hold, give 20 mL/kg saline bolus, commence IV antibiotics covering Gram-negatives and anaerobes, place NG tube on free drainage, then proceed to air-contrast enema reduction by interventional radiology with surgical team scrubbed and available
options.rationale: Pneumatic (air) enema is the first-line treatment in haemodynamically stable children without peritonism, with success rates of 75-90% in Australian centres. Resuscitation, broad-spectrum antibiotics, NG decompression and surgical standby for the 1% risk of perforation during reduction are mandatory (RCH CPG; RANZCR Paediatric Imaging).

options.text: Proceed directly to laparotomy for manual reduction without attempting radiological reduction
options.rationale: Surgery is reserved for failed enema reduction, peritonism, perforation, or a pathological lead point. There is no peritonism here and air enema has higher success and lower morbidity (RCH CPG).

options.text: Perform a contrast CT abdomen to confirm the diagnosis before any intervention
options.rationale: Ultrasound is the gold-standard investigation. CT adds radiation and delay without changing management (RANZCR; RCH CPG).

options.text: Admit for observation overnight on clear fluids and re-image with ultrasound in the morning
options.rationale: Conservative observation of confirmed ileocolic intussusception causes progressive ischaemia. Reduction within 24 hours of symptom onset has the best success rate (RCH CPG).

explanation.summary: Ileocolic intussusception (6 months to 2 years, peak 5-9 months) typically follows a viral illness with Peyer patch hypertrophy as the lead point. Triad of colicky pain, vomiting and red-currant stool is the late presentation. Diagnosis is by USS (target/donut sign on cross-section, pseudokidney sign longitudinally) and reduction is by pneumatic (air) enema.
key_points: Peak age 5-9 months; consider pathological lead point if outside 3 months to 5 years (e.g. Meckel diverticulum, lymphoma, HSP)
Triad (pain, vomiting, blood per rectum) present in less than 50%
USS first-line; air enema first-line treatment
Air enema success 75-90% in Australia; perforation rate less than 1%
Surgery if peritonism, perforation, failed enema or pathological lead point
explanation.pearls: Recurrence after successful enema reduction occurs in approximately 10% and is usually within 24-48 hours. Discharge planning includes return-precaution counselling.

sources.label: RCH Clinical Practice Guideline: Intussusception
RANZCR Paediatric Imaging Guidelines

subtopic: Bilious vomiting in the first 48 hours of life
stem: A 38-hour-old term male born at 39+5 by NVD to a G2P1 mother with an uneventful pregnancy is brought to the special care nursery by the midwife. He has had three episodes of vomiting in the last 90 minutes, the most recent two clearly bilious (bright green) and photographed on the midwife's phone for the registrar to review. He passed meconium at 18 hours of age and has been breastfeeding poorly since the second feed, latching but suckling weakly and falling asleep. Birth weight 3.4 kg, current weight 3.25 kg, representing a 4% drop. On examination he is irritable with HR 178, RR 62, CRT 3 seconds, T 36.4 degC, SpO2 96% in room air. The abdomen is mildly distended in the upper abdomen without obvious mass, soft, with sluggish bowel sounds. There is no inguinal hernia, no anal stenosis, and no perianal abnormality. First-degree relatives have no history of cystic fibrosis or congenital gut malformation. Antenatal scans had not commented on intra-abdominal anatomy beyond reporting a normal anatomy scan at 20 weeks, with no polyhydramnios noted. An NG tube is placed and immediately drains 25 mL of bilious fluid with relief of distension. The neonatal registrar has IV access through a peripheral cannula and is preparing maintenance fluids and a 10 mL/kg saline bolus.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 178, RR 62, T 36.4 degC, SpO2 96% RA, CRT 3 s, weight 3.25 kg.
data_table.Examination: Irritable, mildly distended abdomen, soft, no mass, sluggish bowel sounds. No hernia, normal anus.
data_table.Investigations: NG aspirate 25 mL bilious. IV access x 1, fluids being prepared.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next investigation?

options.text: Upper GI contrast study within the next hour, performed with the paediatric surgical team aware and theatre on standby
options.rationale: Bilious vomiting in a neonate is malrotation with midgut volvulus until proven otherwise. Upper GI contrast is the gold-standard investigation, showing an abnormal duodeno-jejunal junction position and a corkscrew or beak appearance if volvulus is present. This must occur with surgical standby because volvulus requires laparotomy within minutes to hours to prevent loss of the midgut (RCH CPG; ANZNN).

options.text: Conservative management with NG decompression and IV fluids overnight, with reassessment in the morning
options.rationale: Watch-and-wait management of bilious vomiting in a neonate can result in complete loss of the midgut within 4-6 hours of established volvulus. This is a surgical emergency (RCH CPG).

options.text: Abdominal ultrasound looking for whirlpool sign before considering further imaging
options.rationale: USS whirlpool sign (SMA-SMV inversion with twisted mesentery) is supportive but not gold-standard, and absence of the sign does not exclude malrotation. Upper GI contrast remains the definitive investigation (RANZCR; RCH CPG).

options.text: Plain supine abdominal radiograph alone to exclude duodenal atresia
options.rationale: Plain AXR is useful (may show double-bubble for duodenal atresia, or paucity of distal gas in volvulus) but is insufficient to confirm or exclude malrotation. Upper GI contrast is required (RCH CPG).

options.text: Contrast enema looking for a microcolon to exclude meconium ileus
options.rationale: Meconium ileus is a differential but in a term infant who passed meconium at 18 hours it is less likely than malrotation. Upper GI contrast is the priority investigation for bilious vomiting (RCH CPG).

explanation.summary: Bilious vomiting in a neonate is malrotation with volvulus until proven otherwise. The midgut twists around a narrow mesenteric pedicle and can necrose within hours. Upper GI contrast is the gold-standard investigation. The corrective operation is a Ladd procedure: counter-clockwise detorsion, division of Ladd bands, broadening of the mesentery, appendicectomy, and placement of small bowel on the right.
key_points: Any green vomit in the first month is malrotation/volvulus until excluded
Upper GI contrast = gold standard (DJ flexure should be left of midline at the level of pylorus)
USS whirlpool sign is supportive but not definitive
Operation = Ladd procedure
Time from established volvulus to midgut loss may be as short as 4-6 hours
explanation.pearls: If you are ever the most senior person at the bedside of a green-vomiting neonate, your job is to phone the paediatric surgical centre before you have all the answers.

sources.label: RCH Clinical Practice Guideline: Bilious Vomiting in the Neonate
ANZNN Clinical Practice Guidelines

subtopic: School-age child with right iliac fossa pain
stem: An 8-year-old boy presents with a 22-hour history of central abdominal pain that migrated to the right iliac fossa 8 hours ago. He has vomited twice, has not eaten since lunch yesterday, and has a low-grade temperature of 38.1 degC. He is reluctant to walk and held his right hip flexed on arrival. There is no diarrhoea, no urinary symptoms, no foreign travel, no sick contacts. He is not on any medications and is fully immunised. On examination HR 112, RR 22, BP 108/68, abdomen with localised tenderness at McBurney point, voluntary guarding without rebound, positive cough sign. There is no peritonism. Bloods: WCC 14.8 x10^9/L (neutrophils 11.2), CRP 38 mg/L. Urinalysis is negative. A paediatric appendicitis score is calculated at 7/10. The paediatric surgical registrar reviews and the radiology department offers ultrasound within the next hour.

data_table.Examination: Reluctant to walk, McBurney tenderness, voluntary guarding, positive cough sign, no rebound.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Discharge home with simple analgesia and safety-net advice given the equivocal clinical picture
options.rationale: PAS 7/10 with WCC 14.8, CRP 38 and migratory pain is high probability for appendicitis. Discharge is unsafe (RCH CPG Appendicitis).

options.text: Order a contrast CT abdomen and pelvis as the first-line imaging investigation
options.rationale: CT is reserved for diagnostic uncertainty after USS or when USS is non-visualising in older or larger children. Ultrasound is first-line in children to minimise ionising radiation (RANZCR; RCH CPG).

options.text: Proceed to ultrasound of the right iliac fossa; if appendix is visualised and abnormal proceed to laparoscopic appendicectomy with IV antibiotics started preoperatively, and if non-visualising arrange surgical review for serial examination
options.rationale: Ultrasound is first-line imaging in children. A visualised, non-compressible, blind-ending tubular structure greater than 6 mm with hyperaemia is diagnostic and triggers laparoscopic appendicectomy. Non-visualisation occurs in 20-40% and does not exclude appendicitis, so serial clinical reassessment is the next step (RCH CPG; RANZCR).

options.text: Treat with IV cefotaxime and metronidazole as antibiotic-only therapy, avoiding surgery
options.rationale: Antibiotic-only management is a research approach in uncomplicated appendicitis only and is not standard of care in Australian paediatric surgery; appendicectomy remains first-line. Confirmation of appendicitis must precede any non-operative trial (RCH CPG).

options.text: Order an MRI abdomen and pelvis as the first-line investigation to avoid radiation
options.rationale: MRI is not routinely available out of hours in Australia and is not first-line. Ultrasound provides the best speed-to-diagnosis ratio in children (RANZCR).

explanation.summary: Paediatric appendicitis diagnosis is clinical plus imaging. The Paediatric Appendicitis Score (PAS) stratifies: 1-3 low (discharge with safety net), 4-6 intermediate (USS, observe), 7-10 high (surgical review, USS if needed, appendicectomy). USS is first-line imaging; CT is reserved for diagnostic uncertainty after non-diagnostic USS.
key_points: PAS components: anorexia, nausea/vomiting, migration, RIF tenderness, cough/percussion/heel-tap tenderness, fever greater than or equal to 38, WCC greater than 10, neutrophils greater than 7.5
USS first-line: appendix diameter greater than 6 mm, non-compressible, hyperaemic, periappendiceal fluid
Laparoscopic appendicectomy with preoperative IV antibiotics (e.g. cefazolin + metronidazole per Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)
Non-visualising USS does not exclude appendicitis; serial exam by paediatric surgical team
CT reserved for diagnostic uncertainty after USS
explanation.pearls: A child who refuses to climb onto the bed or who walks bent over is telling you the diagnosis.

sources.label: RCH Clinical Practice Guideline: Acute Appendicitis
Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic - Surgical Prophylaxis
RANZCR Paediatric Imaging Guidelines

subtopic: Adolescent with acute unilateral scrotal pain
stem: A 13-year-old boy presents to a regional emergency department at 03:00 with sudden severe left scrotal pain that woke him from sleep 4 hours ago, associated with one episode of vomiting. There is no dysuria, no urethral discharge, no recent trauma, no recent URTI. He is not sexually active. On examination he is in significant pain, the left hemiscrotum is swollen, erythematous and tender, the left testis is high-riding with a transverse lie, and the cremasteric reflex is absent on the left. Prehn sign is negative (lifting the testis does not relieve pain). The right testis is normal. Urinalysis is negative for leucocytes and nitrites. The nearest paediatric surgical service is 90 minutes away by road. The local on-call general surgeon is in-house. Bedside Doppler ultrasound is not immediately available; the sonographer is on call from home and estimates a 45-minute arrival time.

data_table.Examination: Left hemiscrotum swollen, high-riding transverse testis, absent cremasteric reflex, negative Prehn sign.
data_table.Investigations: Urinalysis negative. Doppler USS not available for 45 minutes.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Wait for the on-call sonographer to arrive and perform Doppler ultrasound before any surgical decision
options.rationale: Testicular torsion is a clinical diagnosis and ultrasound must not delay exploration. Salvage rates fall from greater than 90% within 6 hours to less than 10% beyond 24 hours (RCH CPG; USANZ).

options.text: Commence IV ceftriaxone and doxycycline for presumed epididymo-orchitis
options.rationale: The clinical picture (high-riding transverse testis, absent cremasteric reflex, negative Prehn, sudden onset waking from sleep, no urinary symptoms) is torsion not epididymo-orchitis. Antibiotics waste time and miss the salvage window (RCH CPG).

options.text: Proceed immediately to scrotal exploration by the on-call general surgeon, with consent for bilateral orchidopexy plus orchidectomy if non-viable, and notify the paediatric surgical service for advice and follow-up
options.rationale: Testicular torsion is a clinical diagnosis warranting immediate surgical exploration. The salvage window is 4-6 hours from onset of pain (currently 4 hours in this scenario). In regional Australia the on-call general surgeon performs exploration rather than transferring; bilateral orchidopexy is standard because the bell-clapper anatomy is bilateral in approximately 80% (RCH CPG; USANZ Position Statement).

options.text: Attempt manual detorsion in the open-book direction (lateral rotation) then discharge home with urology outpatient follow-up
options.rationale: Manual detorsion may be attempted while preparing for theatre but does not replace surgical exploration and fixation. Discharge without operative fixation risks re-torsion (USANZ).

options.text: Transfer the patient by road to the paediatric surgical service 90 minutes away for definitive management
options.rationale: Adding 90 minutes of transfer time when local in-house surgical capability exists pushes total ischaemic time beyond 6 hours and risks losing the testis. Local exploration with paediatric surgical phone advice is the correct approach (USANZ).

explanation.summary: Testicular torsion is a clinical diagnosis treated by immediate scrotal exploration. Salvage rates depend on duration of ischaemia: greater than 90% within 6 hours, approximately 50% at 12 hours, less than 10% beyond 24 hours. Bilateral orchidopexy is performed because the bell-clapper deformity is bilateral in approximately 80%.
key_points: Hallmarks: sudden onset pain (often wakes from sleep), high-riding transverse testis, absent cremasteric reflex, negative Prehn
Differentials: epididymo-orchitis (usually febrile, dysuria, gradual onset), torsion of appendix testis (blue dot sign, less severe), incarcerated hernia
Imaging should not delay exploration when the clinical picture is clear
Bilateral orchidopexy at exploration
Salvage rate greater than 90% within 6 hours
explanation.pearls: When the question gives you a 6-hour window and an obvious clinical diagnosis, the wrong answers are usually 'order an imaging test' or 'transfer'.

sources.label: RCH Clinical Practice Guideline: Acute Scrotum
Urological Society of Australia and New Zealand (USANZ) Position Statement on Testicular Torsion

subtopic: Neonate with green vomiting on day 1 - differential framework
stem: A 12-hour-old term female born by elective lower-segment caesarean section for breech presentation at 39+2 weeks has now had two episodes of bright-green vomiting after attempted breastfeeding. Antenatal scans at 12 and 20 weeks were reported as normal; nuchal translucency screening was low risk. Birth weight 3.1 kg, Apgars 9 and 9. She has not yet passed meconium. On examination HR 162, RR 56, T 36.8 degC, SpO2 97% in room air. The abdomen is mildly distended in the upper abdomen with a flat lower abdomen and visible epigastric fullness. There is no obvious mass, no inguinal hernia, and the anus is patent on inspection. A nasogastric tube is placed and aspirates 30 mL of bilious fluid. The midwife asks the on-call paediatric registrar to interpret a supine abdominal radiograph.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 162, RR 56, T 36.8 degC, SpO2 97% RA.
data_table.Examination: Upper abdominal distension, flat lower abdomen, patent anus, no hernia. NG bilious aspirate 30 mL.

lead_in: Which finding on the abdominal radiograph would be most consistent with duodenal atresia rather than malrotation with volvulus?

options.text: Multiple dilated small bowel loops with air-fluid levels and a soap-bubble appearance in the right lower quadrant
options.rationale: Soap-bubble (Neuhauser sign) appearance is meconium ileus, not duodenal atresia (RCH CPG).

options.text: Pneumatosis intestinalis with portal venous gas
options.rationale: Pneumatosis and portal venous gas are radiographic signs of necrotising enterocolitis, not duodenal atresia (RCH CPG NEC).

options.text: Double bubble sign with no distal bowel gas
options.rationale: Duodenal atresia produces a classic double bubble (dilated stomach and proximal duodenum) with absent distal bowel gas. It is associated with trisomy 21 in approximately 30% and is the surgical diagnosis least likely to lose bowel acutely. Malrotation with volvulus usually shows a paucity of distal gas with a corkscrew on upper GI contrast (RCH CPG; ANZNN).

options.text: Free intraperitoneal gas under the diaphragm with a football sign
options.rationale: Free gas indicates a perforation, not duodenal atresia (RCH CPG NEC).

options.text: Corkscrew appearance of the duodenum on supine film
options.rationale: Corkscrew is a fluoroscopic finding on upper GI contrast in malrotation with volvulus, not a plain film finding (RCH CPG).

explanation.summary: Bilious vomiting in the neonate is malrotation with volvulus until proven otherwise. The framework of differentials includes malrotation/volvulus, duodenal atresia, jejunal/ileal atresia, meconium ileus (CF), Hirschsprung disease, NEC, sepsis-related ileus, and incarcerated inguinal hernia. Plain radiograph plus upper GI contrast distinguish these.
key_points: Double bubble + no distal gas = duodenal atresia (30% trisomy 21)
Double bubble + distal gas = malrotation with partial obstruction
Multiple dilated loops + soap-bubble = meconium ileus (suspect CF)
Pneumatosis = NEC
Free gas / football sign = perforation
explanation.pearls: If the lower abdomen is scaphoid in a vomiting neonate, suspect a high obstruction; if it is distended, suspect a low obstruction.

sources.label: RCH Clinical Practice Guideline: Bilious Vomiting in the Neonate
ANZNN Clinical Practice Guidelines

subtopic: Toddler with delayed meconium and chronic constipation
stem: A 16-month-old boy of Anglo-Australian background presents with severe constipation since the second week of life. His mother reports he passed meconium at 52 hours of age, required digital stimulation to pass stool for the first 6 months, and was breastfed exclusively until 6 months when solids were introduced uneventfully. He now stools once every 5-7 days, with explosive decompression when finally passing. His growth has crossed centiles downwards from the 50th at birth to the 9th today. He is now febrile at 38.6 degC, lethargic, with foul-smelling green-tinged diarrhoea overnight, mild abdominal distension, and tachycardia at 148. His abdomen is distended and tympanic, with no obvious mass and no organomegaly. PR examination is performed gently by the paediatric registrar and produces an explosive release of gas and liquid stool with relief of distension. He has had multiple aperient trials including macrogol and lactulose without sustained improvement, and dietary fibre optimisation through the maternal and child health nurse. There is no family history of cystic fibrosis or thyroid disease. Newborn screening for cystic fibrosis was negative.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 148, RR 32, T 38.6 degC, weight on 9th centile (was 50th at birth).
data_table.Examination: Distended tympanic abdomen, explosive PR release. Lethargic.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate definitive investigation?

options.text: Sweat chloride test to exclude cystic fibrosis as the cause of meconium delay
options.rationale: CF can cause meconium ileus in the newborn period but does not explain chronic explosive PR decompression, ongoing rectal outlet obstruction, or the current enterocolitis picture. Sweat test may be a differential investigation but is not the definitive test for this presentation (RCH CPG).

options.text: Anorectal manometry alone as the definitive test
options.rationale: Anorectal manometry shows absent recto-anal inhibitory reflex but is technically difficult in young children and is supportive rather than definitive. Biopsy remains gold standard (RCH CPG).

options.text: Contrast enema with assessment for transition zone, followed by trial of laxatives
options.rationale: Contrast enema may show a transition zone and reversed recto-sigmoid ratio but is supportive only. Biopsy is required for diagnosis. Empirical laxatives miss the underlying disease (RCH CPG).

options.text: Full-thickness rectal suction biopsy with assessment for ganglion cells and acetylcholinesterase staining
options.rationale: Rectal biopsy is the gold-standard diagnostic test for Hirschsprung disease, demonstrating absent ganglion cells in the submucosal (Meissner) and myenteric (Auerbach) plexuses with hypertrophied nerve trunks staining positive for acetylcholinesterase. The current presentation is Hirschsprung-associated enterocolitis, which carries up to 30% mortality if untreated (RCH CPG; ANZ Paediatric Surgery).

options.text: Faecal calprotectin and stool MCS as the initial diagnostic test
options.rationale: Faecal calprotectin is useful in suspected IBD, not Hirschsprung disease. Stool MCS may identify enterocolitis pathogens but does not diagnose the underlying aganglionosis (RCH CPG).

explanation.summary: Hirschsprung disease is congenital absence of ganglion cells in the distal bowel due to failed migration of neural crest cells. Suspect in any infant with delayed meconium beyond 48 hours, chronic constipation from birth, or failure-to-thrive. Hirschsprung-associated enterocolitis is the life-threatening complication.
key_points: Meconium passed greater than 48 hours after birth in approximately 90%
Rectal biopsy is gold standard: absent ganglion cells, hypertrophied nerves, positive acetylcholinesterase
Contrast enema: transition zone, reversed recto-sigmoid ratio
Definitive treatment: pull-through procedure (Soave, Swenson, Duhamel)
Hirschsprung-associated enterocolitis: fever, distension, foul diarrhoea; mortality up to 30% if missed
explanation.pearls: An infant who needs digital stimulation to pass meconium is telling you the diagnosis; do not wait for failure-to-thrive.

sources.label: RCH Clinical Practice Guideline: Hirschsprung Disease
Therapeutic Guidelines: Gastrointestinal - Paediatric Constipation

subtopic: Preterm infant with feed intolerance and abdominal distension
stem: A 28+5 week preterm female born at birth weight 1.05 kg is now on day 11 of life in the neonatal intensive care unit. She was extubated to CPAP on day 4 and trophic feeds of expressed breast milk were introduced on day 5 and advanced to 60 mL/kg/day by day 10. Over the last 12 hours she has had two large bilious gastric aspirates, abdominal distension increasing from 25 cm to 28.5 cm girth, and one episode of bright red blood in the stool. She has become apnoeic with three desaturations to less than 80% in the last hour requiring stimulation. HR has risen from 145 to 178. T is 36.0 degC, BP 48/22 mmHg with mean arterial pressure 30. CRT is 4 seconds. The abdominal wall is shiny with bluish discolouration over the right flank. WCC has fallen from 9.2 to 4.1 x10^9/L, platelets from 224 to 86, CRP risen from less than 5 to 92 mg/L, lactate 4.8 mmol/L. The supine abdominal radiograph shows multiple dilated loops of bowel with intramural gas (pneumatosis intestinalis) and a curvilinear lucency over the liver consistent with portal venous gas. There is no free intraperitoneal gas on a left lateral decubitus film. The paediatric surgical service has been consulted by phone.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 178, RR 70, T 36.0 degC, BP 48/22 (MAP 30), SpO2 88% on CPAP, CRT 4 s.
data_table.Examination: Distended shiny abdomen with bluish flank discolouration. PR bright red blood.
data_table.Investigations: WCC 4.1, platelets 86, CRP 92, lactate 4.8. AXR: pneumatosis + portal venous gas, no free gas.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate immediate management?

options.text: Withhold antibiotics until blood culture results are available to avoid promoting antibiotic resistance
options.rationale: Empirical broad-spectrum antibiotics covering Gram-negatives and anaerobes must be commenced immediately on suspicion of NEC. Waiting for cultures delays critical therapy (RCH CPG; Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Continue enteral feeds at current volume and add probiotics to reduce gut translocation
options.rationale: Established NEC mandates immediate NBM. Continuing feeds worsens ischaemic injury and risks perforation. Probiotics are used prophylactically in some units but have no role in established NEC (RCH CPG NEC).

options.text: Proceed immediately to laparotomy regardless of radiographic findings of perforation
options.rationale: Surgery is indicated for perforation (free gas, fixed loop, clinical deterioration despite medical management). Bell IIB without perforation is managed medically with surgical standby (RCH CPG NEC).

options.text: Nil by mouth, 10 Fr orogastric tube on free drainage, IV access, send blood cultures, commence IV benzylpenicillin, gentamicin and metronidazole, 10 mL/kg normal saline bolus, parenteral nutrition through a central line, serial 6-hourly abdominal radiographs including a left lateral decubitus film, and continue paediatric surgical involvement with theatre on standby
options.rationale: This is Bell stage IIB NEC (pneumatosis with portal venous gas, no perforation). Management is medical: NBM, OG decompression, triple antibiotics (penicillin/gentamicin/metronidazole per Therapeutic Guidelines), TPN, serial AXR every 6 hours for 48-72 hours to detect perforation. Surgery is indicated for perforation (Bell IIIB) or clinical deterioration (RCH CPG NEC; ANZNN).

options.text: Place a peritoneal drain at the bedside as definitive treatment without antibiotic therapy
options.rationale: Peritoneal drainage is a salvage technique for unstable extremely-low-birth-weight infants with perforation, not a substitute for antibiotics or definitive laparotomy. There is no demonstrated perforation here (ANZNN).

explanation.summary: Necrotising enterocolitis is a multifactorial inflammatory/ischaemic injury to immature gut, peaking at 28-32 weeks corrected age in the second to third postnatal week. Bell staging: I suspected (non-specific), II definite (IIA pneumatosis, IIB pneumatosis + portal gas), III advanced (IIIA critically ill no perforation, IIIB perforation).
key_points: Peak: 28-32 weeks corrected, day 7-14 of life
Risk factors: prematurity, formula feeding, rapid feed advance, hypoxia, sepsis
Pneumatosis intestinalis is pathognomonic
Management: NBM, OG decompression, triple antibiotics, TPN, serial AXR
Surgery for perforation, fixed dilated loop, abdominal wall erythema/discolouration, or deterioration
explanation.pearls: A shiny abdomen with bluish discolouration is a late sign of full-thickness bowel wall ischaemia and is a call to the operating room.

sources.label: RCH Clinical Practice Guideline: Necrotising Enterocolitis
ANZNN Clinical Practice Guidelines
Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic - Neonatal Sepsis

subtopic: Toddler with witnessed coin-like object ingestion
stem: A 22-month-old girl is brought to a rural emergency department 70 minutes after her older brother saw her swallow a small shiny disc that he had taken from a household remote control. He believes she put it directly in her mouth and swallowed it. She is now drooling slightly, refuses to drink, has had one episode of non-bloody vomiting but is not in respiratory distress. There is no stridor, no wheeze, no blood in the saliva. HR 134, RR 28, T 36.9 degC, SpO2 99% in room air. Examination of the oropharynx is unremarkable; the abdomen is soft and non-tender. The mother brings the empty battery compartment of the remote and the parents identify the missing battery as a CR2032 lithium coin cell (20 mm diameter). The emergency department has plain radiography but no on-site paediatric endoscopy. The nearest paediatric centre with interventional endoscopy is 2 hours by road or 45 minutes by retrieval helicopter (currently available). A chest and abdominal radiograph shows a round radio-opaque disc with a double-rim (halo) appearance at the level of T2-T3 in the oesophagus.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 134, RR 28, T 36.9 degC, SpO2 99% RA.
data_table.Examination: Drooling, refuses fluids, no stridor, no respiratory distress.
data_table.Investigations: CXR/AXR: 20 mm disc with double-rim sign at T2-T3 oesophagus.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Transfer by road ambulance over 2 hours without any oral honey, as honey is not safe in a child under 24 months
options.rationale: The honey contraindication is for infants under 12 months due to botulism risk; this child is 22 months. Helicopter is faster than 2-hour road transfer for an oesophageal battery (RCH CPG; NHMRC Infant Feeding).

options.text: Discharge home with safety-net advice to return if symptoms worsen, given the child is currently stable
options.rationale: Any button battery in the oesophagus is a surgical emergency regardless of current symptoms. Delay risks perforation, tracheo-oesophageal fistula and aorto-oesophageal fistula (RCH CPG; ACCC product safety alerts).

options.text: Induce vomiting with ipecac syrup at the bedside
options.rationale: Induced emesis is contraindicated due to risk of aspiration and further oesophageal injury, and ipecac is no longer recommended by the Australian Poisons Information Centre for any ingestion (PIC).

options.text: Wait 4 hours and repeat radiograph to see whether the battery has progressed past the lower oesophageal sphincter
options.rationale: Watchful waiting is unsafe for an oesophageal battery. The 4-hour delay window pushes the child beyond the 2-hour necrosis threshold and risks fistulation (RCH CPG).

options.text: Activate retrieval to the paediatric centre by helicopter for endoscopic removal within 2 hours of ingestion, give honey 10 mL orally every 10 minutes en route in a child over 12 months with intact airway, do not allow oral intake otherwise, and notify Poisons Information Centre on 13 11 26
options.rationale: Button battery in the oesophagus is a time-critical emergency: tissue injury begins within 15-30 minutes and full-thickness necrosis can occur within 2 hours. The Australian Resuscitation Council and RCH CPG recommend honey (or sucralfate) every 10 minutes in children over 12 months with no airway compromise to coat the battery and reduce alkaline injury, while urgent endoscopic removal is arranged. Helicopter retrieval is faster than road (RCH CPG; Poisons Information Centre).

explanation.summary: Button battery in the oesophagus causes alkaline electrolysis injury within 15-30 minutes and can perforate within 2 hours. Australian guidelines (RCH, Poisons Information Centre, ACCC) recommend immediate endoscopic removal, honey every 10 minutes in children over 12 months while awaiting endoscopy, and notification of PIC on 13 11 26.
key_points: Double-rim (halo) sign on AP radiograph distinguishes battery from coin
Step-off sign on lateral
Oesophageal lodgement = endoscopic removal within 2 hours
Honey 10 mL every 10 minutes (children over 12 months) reduces injury severity
Avoid in infants under 12 months (botulism); avoid if airway compromised
explanation.pearls: Coins are smooth and round and easy to ignore. The double rim on the AP film and step-off on the lateral are how you do not miss the diagnosis.

sources.label: RCH Clinical Practice Guideline: Button Battery Ingestion
Poisons Information Centre Australia (13 11 26)
ACCC Product Safety Australia - Button Batteries

subtopic: Infant with vomiting, eczema and stool changes
stem: A 4-month-old formula-fed infant is brought to the GP with a 6-week history of frequent posseting, irritability and back-arching after feeds, and intermittent streaks of fresh blood and mucus in the stool that the mother has photographed on her phone. He has mild atopic eczema on the cheeks and antecubital fossae. He was born at term, weight at birth 3.5 kg (50th centile). At 6 weeks his weight was on the 50th centile; today at 4 months he is on the 9th centile, having crossed two major centiles downward over 10 weeks. He is exclusively fed standard cow-milk-based formula and has never had any breastmilk feeds since the second week of life. There is a family history of asthma in the mother and peanut allergy in an older sibling. He is otherwise well, with no fever, no dehydration, normal urine output, and a normal abdominal examination with no distension or tenderness. The mother has tried thickened formula, smaller more frequent feeds, and head-up positioning after feeds for 30 minutes, without improvement.

data_table.Examination: Mild facial eczema. Soft non-tender abdomen. Weight crossed from 50th to 9th centile over 10 weeks.
data_table.Investigations: Stool with visible blood and mucus streaks. No fever.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Commence oral omeprazole 1 mg/kg daily for a 4-week trial as the most likely diagnosis is GORD
options.rationale: Blood and mucus in stool, eczema, atopic family history and weight faltering are red flags for cow's milk protein allergy rather than uncomplicated GORD. PPI trials should not delay an elimination diet trial when CMPA is suspected (RCH CPG; ASCIA).

options.text: Switch to an extensively hydrolysed formula for a 2 to 4 week trial, monitor symptoms and growth, and if there is clear improvement plan a re-challenge after at least 4 weeks to confirm the diagnosis; refer to paediatric allergy if symptoms severe or persistent
options.rationale: Suspected non-IgE-mediated CMPA in a formula-fed infant is managed by a trial of extensively hydrolysed formula (eHF) for 2-4 weeks, with reassessment of symptoms and growth, and a re-challenge to confirm. Amino-acid formula is reserved for severe or eHF-non-responsive cases. Diagnosis is by elimination and re-challenge, not allergy testing (ASCIA; RCH CPG).
source_refs: RCH CPG Cow's Milk Protein Allergy

options.text: Switch directly to soy-based formula as the first-line alternative formula
options.rationale: Soy formula is not recommended as first-line in infants under 6 months due to phytoestrogen concerns and 10-15% cross-reactivity in CMPA. Extensively hydrolysed formula is first-line (ASCIA; NHMRC).

options.text: Order skin-prick testing and specific IgE to cow's milk to confirm the diagnosis before any dietary change
options.rationale: Non-IgE-mediated CMPA (the most likely subtype here) is not detected by IgE or skin-prick testing. Diagnosis is by elimination and re-challenge (ASCIA).

options.text: Advise the mother to dilute the formula with extra water and add infant gripe water at each feed
options.rationale: Diluting formula reduces caloric and electrolyte intake and worsens faltering growth. Gripe water has no role in CMPA (NHMRC Infant Feeding).

explanation.summary: Distinguishing GORD from CMPA in a vomiting infant requires assessment for red flags: blood/mucus in stool, eczema, atopic family history, perianal excoriation, and weight faltering favour CMPA. Uncomplicated GORD without red flags is managed with feed positioning, thickeners and reassurance.
key_points: Non-IgE CMPA: bloody/mucousy stool, eczema, faltering growth, no anaphylactic features; skin-prick/IgE negative
IgE CMPA: urticaria, angioedema, anaphylaxis within 2 hours of ingestion; skin-prick/IgE positive
First-line formula: extensively hydrolysed (eHF) for 2-4 weeks
Amino-acid formula for severe disease or eHF non-response
Re-challenge to confirm diagnosis; most resolve by 3-5 years
explanation.pearls: Bloody mucus + eczema + crossed centiles in a vomiting infant is CMPA until proven otherwise. PPIs do not fix that.

sources.label: ASCIA Cow's Milk (Dairy) Allergy Guidelines
RCH Clinical Practice Guideline: Cow's Milk Protein Allergy
NHMRC Infant Feeding Guidelines

stem: A 10-week-old infant is brought to the emergency department by his mother because of poor feeding for the last 24 hours. He is not yet rolling and is not yet sitting. Examination is unremarkable except for a 2 cm bluish bruise over the left cheek and a 1 cm bruise on the right ear pinna. The mother offers no clear mechanism but mentions that her partner reported the baby may have rolled off the couch, although the baby has never previously rolled. On further examination there is a small frenular tear under the upper lip and the anterior fontanelle is full but not tense. He is afebrile with normal vital signs. His older sibling, aged 3, was treated for a spiral femur fracture last year that was attributed to a fall from a slide. The family is known to community services for housing instability. The mother has a history of intimate-partner violence in a previous relationship. The current partner is not the biological father and is not present. Birth was at term, NVD, with no perinatal concerns, and the baby is up to date with immunisations.

data_table.Examination: 2 cm cheek bruise, 1 cm ear pinna bruise, frenular tear, full fontanelle. Pre-mobile.
data_table.Investigations: Pending. Family known to community services.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Notify the police but not the statutory child protection service, as bruising alone does not meet child protection thresholds
options.rationale: Mandatory reporting in all Australian jurisdictions requires notification to the statutory child protection service (DCJ in NSW, DFFH in VIC, DOCS-equivalents elsewhere). Police notification is additional, not a substitute (AIFS Mandatory Reporting; DCJ NSW).

options.text: Reassure and discharge home with feeding advice, given that the bruises are small and the mother provided a plausible explanation
options.rationale: Any bruise in a pre-mobile infant is a red flag. The TEN-4 FACES rule mandates investigation. Discharge without workup misses an estimated 30% of subsequent more severe abuse (RCH CPG; AIFS).

options.text: Order coagulation studies in isolation to exclude haemophilia and discharge if normal
options.rationale: Coagulation studies are part of the workup but cannot replace skeletal survey, neuroimaging, fundoscopy and statutory notification. Bleeding disorders and NAI are not mutually exclusive (RCH CPG).

options.text: Admit for full child protection workup including skeletal survey, non-contrast CT head, dilated fundoscopy by ophthalmology, FBC, coagulation studies, LFTs, lipase, urine drug screen, and immediate notification to the relevant statutory child protection service (DCJ in NSW; equivalent body in other jurisdictions); plan safe placement before any discharge
options.rationale: Bruising in a pre-mobile infant is a sentinel injury for non-accidental injury (TEN-4 FACES rule: bruising on Torso, Ears, Neck, or any bruising in a child 4 months or younger). Multiple bruises plus a frenular tear plus an unwitnessed mechanism plus a sibling with a suspicious historic fracture meet threshold for mandatory reporting and a full medical workup including skeletal survey, neuroimaging, fundoscopy and coagulation panel (RCH CPG Child Abuse; AIFS; DCJ NSW Mandatory Reporter Guide).

options.text: Wait 48 hours to see if the bruising evolves before deciding whether to report
options.rationale: Mandatory reporting requires action on reasonable belief, not certainty. Waiting risks further injury, escalates risk to siblings and breaches the legal threshold (AIFS).

explanation.summary: The TEN-4 FACES rule (Pierce 2010, validated 2021) identifies sentinel bruising patterns: bruising on Torso, Ear, or Neck in any child 4 years or younger, OR any bruising in an infant 4 months or younger, OR bruising in the FACES regions (Frenulum, Auricular, Cheek, Eye, Sclera). Workup: skeletal survey, CT head, fundoscopy, FBC, coagulation, LFTs, lipase, urine drug screen, social/safety assessment.
key_points: Any bruise in a pre-mobile infant requires NAI workup
TEN-4 FACES: T (torso) E (ear) N (neck) under 4 yr; any bruise under 4 months; FACES regions
Frenular tear in pre-mobile infant is classic NAI sentinel
Mandatory notification to statutory child protection service (DCJ NSW or jurisdictional equivalent)
Mandatory reporters have legal immunity for good-faith reports
explanation.pearls: If a baby cannot roll, they cannot bruise themselves. Document the absence of a developmental match between mechanism and injury.

sources.label: RCH Clinical Practice Guideline: Child Abuse
Australian Institute of Family Studies - Mandatory Reporting of Child Abuse and Neglect
DCJ NSW Mandatory Reporter Guide

stem: A general practitioner working in regional New South Wales sees an 8-year-old girl for a sore throat. During the consultation, while her mother is in the waiting room, the girl mentions that her mother's partner sometimes hits her with a belt when he is drinking and that her younger brother gets hit too. There are two faint linear marks on her back. She asks the GP not to tell anyone because she is worried it will make things worse at home. The GP has known the family for several years. The GP is unsure whether the legal threshold for mandatory reporting has been met, what the consequences are for the doctor-patient relationship, and what immunity is available if the report is later disputed by the family.
lead_in: Which statement most accurately reflects the GP's legal obligation in New South Wales?

options.text: The GP must report to the Department of Communities and Justice if there is a reasonable belief that the child is at risk of significant harm, and the GP is granted legal immunity for good-faith reports under the Children and Young Persons (Care and Protection) Act 1998 (NSW)
options.rationale: In NSW, medical practitioners are mandatory reporters under the Children and Young Persons (Care and Protection) Act 1998. The threshold is reasonable belief that a child is at risk of significant harm. Reports are made to DCJ (formerly FaCS/DOCS). The Act grants immunity from civil, criminal or disciplinary action for good-faith reports (s 29) (DCJ NSW; AIFS).

options.text: The GP must obtain the child's consent before making any report to the statutory child protection service
options.rationale: Mandatory reporting overrides confidentiality and does not require the child's consent. The child's wishes are documented and considered in safety planning but do not block reporting (DCJ NSW).

options.text: Reporting is only required if the child has visible injuries requiring medical treatment
options.rationale: The threshold is risk of significant harm, not severity of current injury. Disclosure of physical abuse by a household member meets threshold regardless of injury severity (DCJ NSW; AIFS).

options.text: The GP should first discuss the concerns with the mother to allow her to address the situation before making any report
options.rationale: Discussing with the alleged non-protective parent before reporting risks tipping off the perpetrator and increasing harm to the child. The report should be made first and discussion with caregivers occurs only after DCJ guidance (RACGP; DCJ NSW).

options.text: Mandatory reporting obligations apply only to teachers and police, not to general practitioners
options.rationale: Mandated professions in NSW include medical practitioners, nurses, midwives, dentists, teachers, early childhood educators, police, and others (DCJ NSW; AIFS).

explanation.summary: Australian mandatory reporting laws are jurisdiction-specific. NSW threshold is reasonable belief of risk of significant harm under the Children and Young Persons (Care and Protection) Act 1998. Medical practitioners are mandated reporters in all Australian jurisdictions. Reports attract statutory immunity for good-faith disclosures.
key_points: NSW threshold: reasonable belief of risk of significant harm
Mandated professions vary by state but include doctors in all jurisdictions
Report to DCJ (NSW), DFFH (VIC), DCP (SA), DCJ (QLD: Child Safety), DCP (WA), CYPS (ACT), Communities (NT), Strong Families Safe Kids (TAS)
Statutory immunity for good-faith reports
Use DCJ Mandatory Reporter Guide decision tool when uncertain
explanation.pearls: The threshold is reasonable belief, not proof. If you can articulate why you are worried, you have met the threshold.

sources.label: DCJ NSW Mandatory Reporter Guide
Australian Institute of Family Studies - Mandatory Reporting
RACGP Abuse and Violence: Working with our patients in general practice (White Book)

stem: A 15-year-old girl attends a GP clinic alone requesting the combined oral contraceptive pill. She is sexually active with one partner who is also 15 and is in the same year at school. She has had a normal menarche at 12, regular cycles every 28-30 days, no medical comorbidities, no migraine with aura, no smoking history, no thrombophilia or family history of VTE, and a normal blood pressure of 110/68. Her BMI is 22. She does not want her parents told because she is worried they will react badly even though she trusts them on most other matters. She lives at home with both parents and an older sister, attends school regularly, is performing well academically, plays competitive netball, and has no signs of coercion, grooming, intimate-partner violence, mental illness, or substance misuse on a HEEADSSS assessment conducted by the GP at this consultation. She asks the GP directly whether the GP must tell her parents, and demonstrates a clear understanding of how the pill works, the failure rate, the risk of VTE, and the importance of barrier protection for STI prevention. Her partner is not present and has not been mentioned in any concerning way.
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate response by the GP?

options.text: Defer all decisions until a Medicare-rebated specialist adolescent gynaecology review, which typically takes 3 to 6 months
options.rationale: GPs are appropriately trained and authorised to prescribe contraception to competent adolescents. Delay exposes the patient to unintended pregnancy (RACGP; Therapeutic Guidelines).
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines: Sexual and Reproductive Health

options.text: Decline to prescribe and require the patient to return with a parent before any consideration of contraception
options.rationale: Mandating parental involvement breaches the patient's right to confidential health care and is likely to drive the patient away from health services. Gillick competence allows confidential prescribing (RACGP).

options.text: Mandatorily notify DCJ because any sexual activity by a 15-year-old is reportable in all Australian jurisdictions
options.rationale: The age of consent in NSW is 16, but consensual sexual activity between two 15-year-olds of similar maturity, without exploitation, coercion or significant age gap, does not automatically meet mandatory reporting threshold. The HEEADSSS-confirmed absence of grooming or coercion is critical. Use the DCJ Mandatory Reporter Guide for the specific scenario (DCJ NSW; AIFS).

options.text: Prescribe the COCP but inform the parents by telephone after the consultation for safety reasons
options.rationale: This breaches confidentiality without consent and undermines trust. Disclosure to parents is reserved for risk-of-significant-harm scenarios that the patient is unable or unwilling to address (RACGP; AMA).

options.text: Assess Gillick/mature minor competence, document understanding of the risks, benefits and alternatives, prescribe the COCP without parental notification, encourage but do not require disclosure to a trusted adult, and document the assessment in detail
options.rationale: Under the High Court of Australia decision in Marion's Case (1992), adapting the UK Gillick principle, a mature minor with sufficient understanding and intelligence to fully comprehend a proposed treatment may consent without parental involvement. Contraception in a 15-year-old in a non-coercive relationship of similar age, with no safety concerns, meets the standard for confidential prescribing (RACGP; AMA Code of Ethics).

explanation.summary: Gillick competence (or mature minor doctrine, as applied in Australia after Marion's Case) allows a young person to consent to treatment without parental involvement if they have sufficient understanding and intelligence to fully appreciate what is proposed. Confidentiality is the default; it is breached only when risk of significant harm cannot be otherwise managed.
key_points: Mature minor doctrine: capacity-based, not age-based
HEEADSSS screen: Home, Education/Employment, Eating, Activities, Drugs, Sexuality, Suicide/Mood, Safety
Confidentiality is the default; explain limits at the start of every adolescent consultation
Limits: risk of significant harm to self or others, including coercion, grooming, or significant age gap
Document the competence assessment in detail
explanation.pearls: Explain confidentiality and its limits at the start of every adolescent consultation. That sentence does more for engagement than anything else you say.

sources.label: RACGP Curriculum and Syllabus: Adolescent and Young Adult Health
AMA Code of Ethics
Therapeutic Guidelines: Sexual and Reproductive Health

subtopic: Adolescent with chronic diarrhoea and weight loss
stem: A 14-year-old boy presents to a paediatric outpatient clinic with a 4-month history of crampy lower abdominal pain that is worse after meals, non-bloody loose stool 4 to 6 times daily including overnight episodes that wake him from sleep, intermittent low-grade fevers, recurrent painful mouth ulcers on the inner lip, and 5 kg of weight loss. He has fallen from the 75th to the 25th height centile over the last year and pubertal development is at Tanner 2 when his peers are at Tanner 3 to 4. There is a paternal uncle with Crohn disease and a maternal grandmother with rheumatoid arthritis. On examination he is pale, BMI 17, with mild RIF tenderness, no palpable mass, a perianal skin tag, and a small fistulous opening at the 7 o'clock position with no active discharge. Bloods show Hb 102 g/L, MCV 76 fL, ferritin 8 microg/L, platelets 482, CRP 48 mg/L, ESR 56 mm/hr, albumin 28 g/L. Faecal calprotectin is 1850 microg/g (reference less than 50). Stool MCS and PCR for bacterial pathogens, C. difficile and parasites are negative. Coeliac serology is negative with normal total IgA. He is haemodynamically stable.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 92, RR 18, BP 110/70, T 37.4 degC, BMI 17.
data_table.Examination: Pale, RIF tender, perianal skin tag, fistulous opening at 7 o'clock.
data_table.Investigations: Hb 102, MCV 76, ferritin 8, platelets 482, CRP 48, albumin 28. Faecal calprotectin 1850 microg/g. Coeliac neg, stool MCS neg.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next investigation?

options.text: Trial of an elimination diet for 6 weeks before any invasive investigation
options.rationale: Exclusive enteral nutrition is a recognised first-line induction therapy for Crohn disease, but is started after diagnostic confirmation by endoscopy, not as a substitute for diagnosis (RCH CPG IBD; eviQ).

options.text: Empirical 4-week course of metronidazole and reassessment of calprotectin
options.rationale: Empirical antibiotics without endoscopy delay diagnosis and risk masking disease activity. Stool studies are already negative for treatable infections (RCH CPG IBD).

options.text: Capsule endoscopy as the first-line investigation to assess small bowel mucosa
options.rationale: Capsule endoscopy is reserved for suspected isolated small bowel disease after negative ileocolonoscopy and small bowel imaging, and is contraindicated where there is risk of stricture and capsule retention. It is not first-line (ESPGHAN; RCH CPG).

options.text: CT abdomen and pelvis with contrast as the imaging modality of choice
options.rationale: MR enterography is preferred over CT in paediatric IBD to minimise lifetime ionising radiation exposure given the need for repeated imaging (RANZCR; eviQ).

options.text: Ileocolonoscopy with serial biopsies of terminal ileum and each colonic segment, plus upper GI endoscopy with biopsies, plus MR enterography
options.rationale: The clinical picture (perianal fistula, mouth ulcers, growth failure, raised inflammatory markers, faecal calprotectin greater than 250 microg/g, family history) is highly suggestive of paediatric Crohn disease. The Porto criteria recommend ileocolonoscopy plus upper GI endoscopy with biopsies plus small-bowel imaging (MR enterography preferred over CT for radiation reduction) for diagnosis and disease mapping (RCH CPG IBD; eviQ Paediatric IBD; ESPGHAN Porto criteria).

explanation.summary: Paediatric IBD diagnosis follows the ESPGHAN revised Porto criteria: ileocolonoscopy + upper GI endoscopy + small bowel imaging (MR enterography preferred). Faecal calprotectin greater than 250 microg/g has high sensitivity for mucosal inflammation and is used to triage who needs endoscopy. Induction in Crohn is exclusive enteral nutrition or corticosteroids; biologics (infliximab/adalimumab) for severe or fistulising disease per eviQ.
key_points: Faecal calprotectin greater than 250 microg/g = likely organic inflammation
Porto criteria: ileocolonoscopy + OGD + small bowel imaging
MR enterography preferred over CT in children
Crohn induction: exclusive enteral nutrition (EEN) 6-8 weeks, mucosal healing in approx 80%
Biologics per eviQ for moderate-severe Crohn, fistulising disease, or steroid-dependent disease
explanation.pearls: Perianal disease in an adolescent + mouth ulcers + growth faltering is Crohn until proven otherwise. Look in the mouth and look at the perianal skin.

sources.label: RCH Clinical Practice Guideline: Inflammatory Bowel Disease
eviQ Paediatric Inflammatory Bowel Disease Protocols
ESPGHAN Revised Porto Criteria for Paediatric IBD

subtopic: Toddler with faltering growth and loose stools
stem: A 22-month-old girl is referred by her maternal and child health nurse for faltering growth. Her birth weight was on the 75th centile after an uncomplicated term pregnancy and NVD. She was exclusively breastfed until 6 months when solids were introduced including wheat-based cereals, pasta and bread, with normal progression of feeding milestones over the next several months. She is now on the 9th centile for weight and 25th for height, having crossed two major weight centiles downward over the last 8 months. Her mother reports chronic loose, bulky, pale, foul-smelling stools 3 to 5 times daily that float in the toilet, marked abdominal distension that develops over the course of the day, irritability particularly in the late afternoon, and a recent reduction in appetite. She has had no fevers, no overseas travel, no antibiotic exposure in the last 12 months, and is up to date with all routine immunisations. The paternal grandmother has type 1 diabetes mellitus and an older sibling aged 7 has confirmed coeliac disease diagnosed two years ago on duodenal biopsy. On examination today she is pale, thin, with mild abdominal distension, no organomegaly, no clubbing, and reduced subcutaneous fat over the buttocks and proximal thighs. The GP orders anti-tTG IgA and total IgA as the initial screen. Anti-tTG IgA returns at 230 U/mL (reference less than 20, more than 10 times upper limit), total IgA is 1.6 g/L (normal for age, excluding IgA deficiency), endomysial antibody is positive, and HLA-DQ2 is positive. The mother attends to discuss the results and asks the GP what should happen next. She is keen to start a strict gluten-free diet immediately because she has already done so for the older sibling and knows the supermarket products.

data_table.Examination: Pale, thin, distended abdomen, reduced buttock fat pads, no organomegaly.
data_table.Investigations: anti-tTG IgA 230 U/mL (less than 20), total IgA 1.6 g/L (normal), EMA positive, HLA-DQ2 positive.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Continue a normal gluten-containing diet, refer to paediatric gastroenterology for duodenal biopsy, and only commence a gluten-free diet after histological confirmation; arrange baseline iron studies, vitamin D, calcium and DEXA scan plan; screen first-degree relatives
options.rationale: Australian and ESPGHAN guidelines require duodenal biopsy in symptomatic children with positive serology before commencing a gluten-free diet, unless the ESPGHAN no-biopsy pathway is fully met (anti-tTG IgA greater than 10x upper limit of normal AND positive EMA on a separate sample AND HLA-DQ2/DQ8 positive) and this is delivered in a specialist paediatric gastroenterology setting with full counselling. Starting a gluten-free diet before biopsy can invalidate the histology if the family later wants definitive diagnosis. First-degree relative screening is recommended (RACGP; Coeliac Australia; ESPGHAN).

options.text: Commence a strict gluten-free diet immediately given the strongly positive serology and family history, with no further investigation needed
options.rationale: Initiating a gluten-free diet before biopsy in Australia is not recommended outside the ESPGHAN no-biopsy pathway delivered by paediatric gastroenterology with full criteria met on two separate serology samples. Starting too early can render later confirmatory biopsy non-diagnostic if uncertainty arises (RACGP; Coeliac Australia).

options.text: Discontinue gluten for 4 weeks then perform a gluten challenge with repeat serology and biopsy
options.rationale: Withdrawing gluten before biopsy makes the diagnostic biopsy unreliable. Gluten challenge is reserved for cases where the original diagnosis was made without adequate evidence or there is later diagnostic doubt, and is usually deferred until age 5 or later because of impact on growth (ESPGHAN).

options.text: Repeat anti-tTG IgA in 6 months and only proceed to further investigation if titres remain elevated
options.rationale: A symptomatic child with strongly positive serology, positive EMA, and faltering growth must not have diagnosis delayed by 6 months of repeat serology. Definitive diagnosis enables treatment that drives catch-up growth (RACGP; Coeliac Australia).

options.text: Order an IgG-based gliadin antibody panel and faecal fat studies as the next investigations
options.rationale: IgG anti-gliadin testing has been superseded by anti-tTG and EMA. Faecal fat is rarely used in modern paediatric coeliac diagnosis. Duodenal biopsy is the definitive next step (RACGP; ESPGHAN).

explanation.summary: Coeliac disease in children: screen with anti-tTG IgA plus total IgA (false negative if IgA deficient). Positive serology in a symptomatic child warrants duodenal biopsy via paediatric gastroenterology to confirm Marsh-Oberhuber 2 or higher histology. The ESPGHAN no-biopsy pathway requires anti-tTG IgA greater than 10x upper limit on one sample, positive EMA on a separate sample, HLA-DQ2/DQ8 positive, and a specialist setting. Do not start a gluten-free diet before diagnosis is secured.
key_points: Screen: anti-tTG IgA + total IgA; if IgA deficient use IgG-based serology
Positive serology + symptoms = paediatric gastroenterology referral for biopsy
ESPGHAN no-biopsy pathway: anti-tTG IgA greater than 10x ULN + EMA positive on second sample + HLA-DQ2/DQ8 positive
Maintain gluten in diet until diagnosis confirmed
Screen first-degree relatives; monitor growth, iron, vitamin D, calcium, thyroid function and bone density
explanation.pearls: The most common reason a coeliac diagnosis becomes uncertain is starting the gluten-free diet too early. Wait for the biopsy or for the full no-biopsy criteria.

sources.label: RACGP Coeliac Disease Position Statement
Coeliac Australia Diagnosis Guidelines
ESPGHAN Guidelines for Diagnosing Coeliac Disease 2020
